subject: Official FAQ and Guide for Reading the Rules

posts.body:
Hey everyone! Welcome to the FAQ thread. You'll find lots of useful stuff here.

Latest FAQ
First and foremost, if you're looking for more answers to common questions, you'll find our FAQ here.

Latest Law of Root
If you have a rules question, the first thing I'd recommend is reading the Law of Root, especially the first few couple chapters—they include important context for understanding the rules structure of Root. You can find the latest version of the Law of Root in the Resources section of our site here.

Old Rulings
You can find many answers to questions by searching for a related thread on BGG, or you can start your own and ask away! However, be careful if you’re reading a thread started before June 2018, since you may be reading about a rule that existed in a pre-release version of the game. Likewise, a couple rulings have changed in the 8th Printing / Marauder Expansion because of changes done to support new content; you can find these changes by searching for an asterisk (*) in the FAQ linked above.

Errata, Additions, and Clarifications
You can find an up-to-date document of all Root errata, additions, rules changes, and clarifications here.

Rules of Thumb
We’ve noticed that almost all of the questions that have come up so far can be answered by following a few rules of thumb, which are included in the welcome sheet in the 2nd printing and beyond. In the 2nd printing and beyond, we’ve included the rules of thumb shown below.

Q1. I’d like to do something, and the rules don’t say that I can’t do it. Can I do it?
A1. Within the confines of the action, you can assume yes! We have yet to encounter such an edge case that wasn’t as designed.

Q2. Can another player not consent to a particular action? 
A2. Root has no interactions that require consent. You just do the thing.

Q3. It seems like something should happen, but the rule doesn’t tell me to do that thing. What do I do?
A3. Follow the word of the Law, not your instinct. Trying to interpret the rules beyond the words on the page will get you in trouble fast. 

Q4. A rule uses a specific term or action. Does it also include another, closely related term or action?
A4. Nope, you can assume we do mean only the original term and not any related terms, no matter how closely related.

Q5. How do I know when a given rule overrides another rule?
A5. Besides the cases in the Law in 1.1 Rules Conflicts, if you can fulfill both a general rule and a faction's rule, you must follow both rules. If you cannot fulfill both rules, the faction's rule overrides the general rule.
Do you plan for a revised digital rulebook or a living faq?

Please add to clarifications:

"9.2.9.III Correction on Hostile Status: If you ever remove or cause removal of a warrior of a non-Hostile faction, their relationship marker immediately moves to the Hostile box. This faction is now Hostile."

"9.2.9.III.a Correction on Infamy: Whenever you remove or cause removal of a piece of a Hostile faction in battle, score one victory point. (Add this to points scored for enemy buildings and tokens. Do not score a point for removing the warrior that made the faction Hostile.)"

Without these corrections, the Vagabond only goes Hostile if removing pieces via Strike or Instigate and only gains Infamy when Instigating as Vagrant (in non-Instigate battles, pieces are removed by the person taking the hits, not the one dealing them).

The phrasing is important, especially with the ruling that removing pieces as the Vagrant = choosing what to remove (which makes a firm connection between removal choice and removal that was previously not stated).

Most factions don't score points for removing warriors, only buildings and tokens. For discussion and an example, see this thread: https://boardgamegeek.com/article/29707401#29707401

also, can a player without warriors play an ambush against a battle? For example, if the marquis battles to kill a support token of the woodland alliance, can that player play an ambush if they have no warriors in tat field?

Yup! Thread here: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/2037527/defenseless-ambush

Replect wrote:

There is a specific Setup-Order for all the factions, but what about turn order? In earlier PnP versions there has been one, but it doesn't seem to be the case anymore, is that right? And who begins the first round?

The order is "random" (i.e. based on seating order).

5.1.1:  Step 1: Assign Factions and Starting Player. Assign

one faction to each player using any method.

The viable factions at each player count are listed

in the learning-to-play guide (page 22). Determine

the starting player and seating order randomly.

Each player takes their chosen faction board and

all pieces, as listed on the back of the faction board.

1.4 Game Structure

Each player’s turn consists of three phases: Birdsong,

Daylight, and Evening. After a player completes all

three phases, the next clockwise player begins their

turn. Play continues until one player has won the game

(3.1).

When including the Mechanical Marquise, modify the following steps of setup.

1st Choose Factions. Give the Mechanical Marquise a random seating position, as if it were a human player. One player sitting near the Mechanical Marquise will be responsible for setting it up and playing its turns. The rest of these rules are written from the perspective of this player.

Hmm, okay. I've read that, but have taken it rather like in the sense of which player takes care of MMs turn than the actual turn order. So I guess it's whatever difficulty one chooses then.

Replect wrote:

Hmm, okay. I've read that, but have taken it rather like in the sense of which player takes care of MMs turn than the actual turn order. So I guess it's whatever difficulty one chooses then.

Since turn order is determined only by seating position, "Give the Mechanical Marquise a random seating position" covers it.  If you want to adjust the difficulty by assigning the MM a set seating position (i.e. place in the turn order) instead of a random one, that's up to your group.

Nope! This falls under the definition of placing warriors:

2.5.1 Warriors. If you are prompted to place a warrior, take it from your supply.

Your Officers box is not your supply, so you cannot.

srinlife wrote:

So my question is with the woodland alliance? Can officers be demoted to make room for more warriors. It costs cards to promote them, but it would make sense that they can be demoted. 

For the officer actions, one of them is to llace a warrior. Can that warrior be from the officer supply if that officer has not taken an action?

Warriors cannot be demoted, as there is no action allowing it.  Pieces can only be placed from your supply (and warriors in the officers box are no longer in your supply).

Lizard acolytes and Riverfolk funds are similar (cannot be placed as warriors).

Caedar wrote:

Hey everyone! Here’s the official FAQ for Root. Thankfully, we’ve found few issues so far that require tweaks to rules wording, but we’ve noticed that almost all of the questions that have come up so far can be answered by following a few rules of thumb. 

Q3. It seems like something should happen, but the rule doesn’t tell me to do that thing. What do I do?

A3. Follow the word of the Law, not your instinct. Trying to interpret the rules beyond the words on the page will get you in trouble fast. 

So after the Mechanical Marquise uses a card to recruit, even though it really seems to me as though that card should be discarded, there is nothing in the rules that say to discard that card, so it will stay at the start of the program for the Marquise, and used again next turn?

Cole said this in https://boardgamegeek.com/article/29643374#29643374, but it's not anywhere in the rules, and if you want the FAQ to say "don't do things that aren't in the rules", and you want the orders discarded after being used, the FAQ should say that. I suggested in the thread where Cole said this that it needs to be in the list of errata, but it still isn't in the FAQ.

I need a rule clarification about erie: i started the match with 2 visirs on my decrete, in my first turn i place o e or two cards on a SINGLE decrete so one decrete is empty? my eire goes in turmoil? how it works

Giacomo,

When you resolve the decree, you take one action for each card in it. You go into turmoil if you are unable to fulfill any one of those actions.

If you have not put any cards in a particular column, you just will not take any actions of that type. There is no requirement to put cards in every column.

Hope that helps.

You can add the cards to any decree (or split them if you add two).  You are not limited to just empty columns (so could end up with 2 empty at the end of the first turn).

which suggests that you keep track of points beyond 30 points.

meaning the active player always wins in these situations.

The wording in the rules. What constitutes a game step?

Scenario: Player A and B both has 29VP and player A starts a battle with player B who has 1 warrior and a building. Player A rolls 1 hit and decide to use Brutal Tactics so they can inflict 1 extra hit and take out their building for the last VP, however, in doing so they give player B 1VP during step 2 of battle.

Will player B win immediatly since it's before step 3 where the hits are dealt?

In this case, the victory point is scored when the extra hit is dealt, not when added. You would use the effect of the card in step 2 (since you're counting up all hits before you deal them all at once), but the point gain is tied to "may deal an extra hit" so it doesn't fire until step 3.

Caedar wrote:

In this case, the victory point is scored when the extra hit is dealt, not when added. You would use the effect of the card in step 2 (since you're counting up all hits before you deal them all at once), but the point gain is tied to "may deal an extra hit" so it doesn't fire until step 3.

In that case it would be possible for the defender to gain more than 1VP during the battle. Say the attacker also had only 1 warrior and the defender rolled 1 hit and used Sappers to take out one of the attacker's buldings. Now the active player A would have 30VP and player B would have reached 31VP at the same time.

The player taking the current turn always chooses the order of multiple simultaneous effects (1.1.3), which would cover the triggering of both Brutal Tactics and Sappers, and victory happens immediately when someone hits 30+ VP (3.1), so the attack would still win in this case, since they would (presumably) say that Brutal Tactics would fire first. Brutal Tactics is one effect, not multiple, so that's why the multiple-player tie-breaker exists.

Hey folks! Just wanted to chime in and say I made an app for this: https://root.seiyria.com/. I really wanted to be able to have the Law/FAQ at my fingertips without having to google it so often, and being able to search for what I'm thinking of has been helpful so far for me.

In particular relevance to the thread, though, I ran into a few things while writing it:

- I noticed that there are only 3 vagabond classes listed in the Law. Will the remainder be added?

- I was reading through some of the text and saw under Vagabond#Evening that it says "Your Evening has four steps, as follows." but similar text in other areas reads "Your Birdsong has two steps in the following order." - is this intentional? It seems inconsistent.

- Similarly, Woodland Alliance#Evening says "This phase has two steps in the following order." instead of "Your Evening has two steps in the following order." like other areas. This also seems inconsistent. 

Another question, in the interest of maintaining this, will all versions of the law be published with their differences somewhere, so we can see what changes between versions?

Thanks.

Some factions/phases have restrictions on when the actions are performed, for game balance reasons.

To use the Woodland Alliance as an example, in Daylight, you can mix up the three possible actions (Craft, Mobilize, Train) in any order.  However, in Birdsong, any Revolt(s) must be performed before any uses of Spread Sympathy -- this prevents you from placing sympathy in a new clearing and then immediately revolting before the defender has a chance to respond.

(Offhand, I can't think of a case where it would matter whether the Alliance draws/discards before or after they Move/Recruit/Battle/Organize, but almost all factions have draw & discard as their very last step.)

Caedar wrote:

Vespert wrote:

How about the extra actions? Is the Marquise de Cat allowed to discard bird cards to do extra actions in-between her regular three free actions?

Yes, we've decided to relax this restriction in the fourth printing rulebook.

[3rd printing] 6.5 Daylight

First, you may activate workshops to craft cards from your hand. Then, you may take up to three actions in any order and combination. After taking three actions, you may take extra actions by spending one bird card per extra action.

[4th printing] 6.5 Daylight

First, you may activate workshops to craft cards from your hand. Then, you may take up to three actions in any order and number, and you may take extra actions by spending one bird card per extra action. 

I'd argue that the 4th printing as written doesn't allow you to prepay bird cards to avoid Outrage. I see that the wording has been changed from the 3rd to the 4th edition. However, if your intent is to allow bird cards to be spent up front (and you did say that in the quote so I appreciate the clarity), unfortunately it's just not coming through in the Law. 

'Extra actions' are simply any actions beyond your initial three. (What else could it possibly mean?) An action can only be called extra if you have already taken 3 actions. 'Extra' as a word still carries the weight of 'after', even though it was removed in the update. Regular actions are finite and primary, so extra actions must follow them. If it's my turn and I've yet to take a single action, how could I "take" my 4th action? I need to take my 1st action first. 

If your argument is that you can pay for an extra action before you need to take it, well, that's not what the Law says. It says "you may take extra actions". It doesn't say to 'prepay an extra action' or 'gain an extra action' or 'queue an action in your pool' or 'increase the number of actions you may take this turn by one'. It only says "take". Take means initiate or execute, and actions are concrete events that happen at a specific time. I can't 'take' an action later. Unless of course, you make your intent explicit in 6.5.

Generally speaking, you can't just discard cards, as much as you might want to just ditch that fox card rather than giving it to the Woodland Alliance. You also can't pay bird cards to take regular actions, only your extra actions. Without a note on timing or some sort of loosely defined action pool, it's simply not explicit on if you can avoid Outrage.

Before I get berated about not following the Law, it's worth restating that this rule was just changed. Either it was written imperfectly in the first place or the intent changed over time. Also, rules can be improved for clarity! There's some ambiguity here.

awesomefresh wrote:

'Extra actions' are simply any actions beyond your initial three. (What else could it possibly mean?) An action can only be called extra if you have already taken 3 actions. 'Extra' as a word still carries the weight of 'after', even though it was removed in the update. Regular actions are finite and primary, so extra actions must follow them. If it's my turn and I've yet to take a single action, how could I "take" my 4th action? I need to take my 1st action first. 

I disagree with this; "Extra" doesn't imply "after" - it just implies "more".

I think the way to look at it is that you have a pool of 3 available actions.  You can spend Bird cards to get more added to that pool; you don't have to have spent the original 3 first.

I have $3 I want to spend on things.  I spend $1 on candy, and I know I want to spend $1 on a book and a newspaper.  Before I do, though, I get another $1; that $1 is extra, even before I spend my original money.  (Not a perfect analogy, of course, but close enough).

The way the 4th printing is worded is admittedly not as crystal clear as it could be.  But 6.5 says "Then, you may take up to three actions in any order and number, and you may take extra actions by spending one bird card per extra action."  It doesn't say "and then you may take extra actions...".  Again, I agree it could be clearer, but I also don't agree that the wording must imply that those extra actions come chronologically only after spending the original 3.  To be honest, I actually had a similar interpretation as you originally, but once the official ruling came in about it (above), I am OK with it - that ruling does match the rules as written, at least in a different way of interpreting the sentence.
I am all for interpreting it as an action pool, but the Law lacks that sort of language.

awesomefresh wrote:

I am all for interpreting it as an action pool, but the Law lacks that sort of language.

Understood, but the Law also doesn't say the Extra actions come after the regular ones, either.  It just says three actions, and extra actions.  Order and timing between those is not specified in the Law.

While I do agree this phrasing isn't clear, that unclearness means there are multiple valid interpretations, so the ruling above clarifies which of those interpretations is the intended one.  The ruling does not contradict the Law.
I take responsibility for the unclarity in the newly written rule. Yes, you can interpret it as an "action pool"—by spending bird cards, you add actions to your pool.

theseraff wrote:

Could you explain me 1.1.4?
LoR 1.1.4 wrote:

Use of Treat. If you are prompted to treat one thing as another, the former takes on all the properties of the latter. 

For example, I hire Riverfolk as mercenaries and I have to do battle in a clearing with Riverfolk and WA sympathy token. Who does pay the card?

You do.  You treat the Riverfolk mercenaries as yours, so they take on the properties of YOUR soldiers.  Thus the Riverfolk player/faction is not battling, YOU are (and hence YOU pay for any removal), even if you have none of your owned soldiers are present.

Similarly, if the Alliance hired the Riverfolk, they would still take the higher die roll (since they are treated as soldiers of the Alliance).  But if the Vagabond battles with Alliance soldiers, the Vagabond does NOT take the higher die rolls, since the soldiers act as Vagabond soldiers and not Alliance soldiers.

Outrage is worded in such a way that the actor (the one doing the "removing" or "moving") pays the cost. Thus, the Vagabond pays the cost because the Vagabond moves the Allied pieces, etc. 

Interestingly, this might also apply when the Vagabond moves Alliance warriors into a sympathetic clearing. I'm not sure whether the Vagabond would be "another player" or not in this case and it says "any warriors" instead of "non-Alliance warriors".

Yes, the rule still applies. Thematically, the Alliance warriors appear (or are, depending on how you look at it) co-opted by the Vagabond.

This is addressed in the first post. I've pasted it below.

Q5. How do I know when a given rule overrides another rule?
A5. Besides the cases in the Law in 1.1 Rules Conflicts, if you can fulfill both a general rule and a faction's rule, you must follow both rules. If you cannot fulfill both rules, the faction's rule overrides the general rule.

...

Example 2: As the Lizard Cult, can I use Convert or Sanctify to only remove a warrior or building, but not place my own warrior or building there? Section 2.5 says if I can't place the amount of a piece prompted, I just place the maximum amount possible, which could be 0.
— Nope! The Lizard's rules in 10.4.3 say, "If you perform Convert or Sanctify, you must complete the conspiracy." This rule conflicts with 2.5, and you cannot fulfill both rules, so the more specific rule ("...you must complete the conspiracy.) wins.
